english: *lam šàn-têr-la ča dug* (lam shan ter la cha dug) the road has an oblique direction. — 2. C.: place of passing over a river.

1. also bšàn-pa (bshan pa), slaughterer, butcher Glr., sometimes also hangman; šàn-kań (shan khang) slaughter-house, butcher's shop, šàn-gri (shan gri) butcher's knife, šàn-grib (shan grib) pollution by the sin of slaughtering an animal. — 2. master or rower of a boat, boatman.

1. W. whispering, *šàb-šùb táń-če (shab shub tang ce), zér-če* (zer ce) to whisper. — 2. also šàb-šób (shab shob) lie, falsehood, šàb-šób byéd-pa (shab shob byed pa) to lie, to cheat; šàb-šúb-čan (shab shub can) deceitful, fraudulent, crafty.

the lower part of a thing, e.g. of a country, šàm-pa (sham pa) a lowlander (opp. to yžúń-pa (gzhung pa) and stód-pa (stod pa)); yšám-du (gsham du) adv. and postp. below, at foot, ráń-leui šám-du °čad (rang le'u'i sham du 'chad) they will be treated of in their respective chapters Lt.; deí šàm-du (de'i sham du) under it, underneath (e.g. to write); šàm-gós (sham gos), šàm-tábs (sham thabs), resp. sku-šám (sku sham) a garment like a petticoat, worn by Tibetan priests and monks.
flounces, fringes, trimmings.

Ssk., in pure Tibetan bde-°byúń (bde 'byung), n. of a fabulous country in the north west of Tibet, fancied to be a kind of paradise; šám-bha-lai lám-yig (sham bha la'i lam yig) (not passport, but:) 'guide for the journey to Shambhala'.
(from šár-ba (shar ba)) 1. east, šar-pyógs (shar phyogs) id.; šar-pa (shar pa) inhabitant of an eastern country; šar-lhó (shar lho) south-east. — 2. termin. of ša (sha), into the flesh.
1. young men, grown-up youth (collective noun) W.; perh. also: a young man. — 2. v. the preceding article.
1. W. adulterer, *šár-po čó-če (shar po co ce) or kúr-če* (khur ce) to commit adultery, (on the part of the husband.) — 2. = šar-pa (shar pa) 1.
a young man, šár-ṗo yzón-nu ysum (shar pho gzon nu gsum) three young men Mil.
pf. and secondary form of °čár-ba ('char ba).
1. Sch.: a strip Schr. šar rgyáb-pa (shar rgyab pa) to sew in long stitches, to baste (Sch.: zuńs ydúb-pa (zungs gdub pa)). — 2. W., C. grown-up girls (collective noun); a female (?)
adulteress, cf. šár-po (shar po).
straightway, directly, šar-šár yró-ba (shar shar 'gro ba) Cs.
Ld. furrowed, having small elevations and hollows.
in rna-šál (rna shal) ear-lap, tip of the ear.
1. Sch. stone-pavement. — 2. a harrow, šál-šal-ba (shal shal ba) Sch., *šál-la dúd-če* (shal la dud ce) Ld. to harrow.
Cs.: a flint, sharp-edged stone; W.: stony ground; mountain side consisting of detritus; šál-ma-čan (shal ma can) full of sharp stones Cs.
Ssk. the seven-leaved silk-cotton tree, Bombax heptaphyllum Stg.

1. part, ča-šas (cha shas) id.; °bras de-las šas yčig ('bras de las shas gcig) part of this rice Dzl.; šas-šás-su bgó-ba (shas shas su bgo ba) to distribute, ... la among Dzl.; šas-čé-ba (shas che ba) a good deal, much, the greater part of, zla-mtsán šas-čé-bai k̇u-k̇rág (zla mtshan shas che ba'i khu khrag) generative fluid in which uterine blood predominates (cf. k̇u-k̇rág (khu khrag) in k̇ú-ba (khu ba)) Wdn.; yti-mug šas-čé-bar gyúr-ba (gti mug shas che bar gyur ba) excess of dullness or stupidity Thgr.; šas-čér (shas cher), šas-čés (shas ches), šas-čén (shas chen) in an eminent degree, in an exceeding measure. — 2. some, a few, žag-šás (zhag shas) some days Mil.; °ga-šas ('ga' shas) some, a few Mil. — 3. instr. of ša (sha).

pf. and secondary form of °či-ba ('chi ba). 1. vb. to die, to expire, to go out (as light, fire); ši-bar gyur-pa-las (shi bar gyur pa las) when she was in a dying state Pth., ši-žíń-pai °óg-tu (shi zhing pa'i 'og tu) after her death; *ši-te lóg(-yoń)-če* (shi te log(-yong)-ce) W. to rise again from the dead, *láń-če* (lang ce) (lit. slań-čes (slang ces)) to raise from the dead. — 2. sbst. the state of dying, expiring, ši-ba-las sos (shi ba las sos) awakened from a dying state frq.; cf. also comp — 3. partic. and adj. ši-ba sós-par byéd-par gyur (shi ba sos par byed par gyur) one already dying still recovers Do; ši-bai lus (shi ba'i lus) the body of the deceased Do.
